title: Tapping (EFT) for Kundalini Crisis: Does It Work?
meta_description: You're here because you're scared, aren't you? It's 3 AM, your body's humming, buzzing, maybe twitching, your mind's racing with thoughts that feel too big
body:
Tapping (EFT) for Kundalini Crisis: Does It Work?

You're here because you're scared, aren't you? It's 3 AM, your body's humming, buzzing, maybe twitching, your mind's racing with thoughts that feel too big, too dark, too... everything. You've probably typed "kundalini crisis help" into Google a hundred times, hoping for some magic bullet, some secret handshake that'll make this all stop. You're looking for anything, right? Anything to pull you back from the edge of whatever this is. And now you're wondering about tapping, EFT. Does it work? Can it help you when you feel like you're losing your mind, or worse, your soul?

Look, I've been there. I've been in that dark, lonely place where every sensation feels like a threat, every thought a precursor to madness. I know that terror, that deep sense of isolation. I know the feeling of being utterly overwhelmed by something you didn't ask for, something you didn't even know existed until it ripped through your life. So, when you ask me if tapping works for kundalini crisis, I'm not going to give you some airy-fairy, spiritual bypass answer. I'm going to tell you what I know, what I've seen, and what might actually give you a shred of peace.

First off, let's get one thing straight: kundalini crisis isn't a walk in the park. It's not just "bad vibes" or a "spiritual detox." It's your entire energetic system, your nervous system, your psyche, getting blown wide open. It's intense. It's disorienting. It can feel like you're dying, going insane, or both. You're not alone in feeling this way. Trust me on that. There's a reason books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) exist. People have been through this, and they've survived. You can too.

What Even Is Tapping (EFT) Anyway?

Okay, so you're probably vaguely familiar with tapping. It's formally called Emotional Freedom Techniques (EFT). It involves tapping on specific meridian points on your face and body - like on your eyebrow, side of your eye, under your eye, under your nose, chin, collarbone, underarm, and top of your head - while you focus on a specific problem or feeling. The idea is that these tapping points are connected to your body's energy system, and by stimulating them, you can release emotional blockages or calm your nervous system. Sounds a bit woo-woo, right? Especially when you're already dealing with something as woo-woo and terrifying as kundalini.

But here's the thing: it's not magic, and it's not just about "positive thinking." It's about interrupting patterns. It's about giving your nervous system a different signal. When you're in the throes of a kundalini crisis, your nervous system is on fire. It's stuck in fight-or-flight, or maybe even freeze mode. You're hyper-vigilant, anxious, probably having panic attacks, and your body feels like it's constantly vibrating or being electrocuted. You're probably not sleeping, you're not eating, and you're just trying to hold on.

EFT, at its core, is a self-regulation tool. It's something you can do yourself, right now, in the middle of the night when you feel like no one understands. It's accessible, and that's a huge deal when you're feeling utterly helpless. You don't need a guru, a shaman, or even a therapist present to do it. You just need your fingers.

How Tapping Connects to Your Nervous System

Think of it this way: when you're in a kundalini crisis, your amygdala - the fear center of your brain - is basically screaming at full volume. It's interpreting every new sensation, every strange thought, as a massive threat. Your nervous system is in overdrive. Tapping, even if you don't believe in "meridians," provides a gentle, rhythmic stimulation that can send a different signal to your brain. It's like telling your amygdala, "Hey, calm down. We're okay. We're just tapping."

It's a form of somatic processing, meaning it helps you process emotions and experiences through your body. And when your body is where so much of the kundalini energy is manifesting - the heat, the vibrations, the involuntary movements - having a tool that addresses the body directly can be incredibly grounding. It's not going to stop the kundalini energy itself - that's a whole different beast you're learning to live with - but it can absolutely help with the anxiety, the fear, the panic, and the feeling of being utterly out of control that comes with it. That's a huge win, right?

My Experience with Tapping During Crisis

I'll tell you what, when I was in the thick of my own spiritual emergency, I tried everything. And I mean everything. I was desperate. Someone suggested tapping to me, and honestly, I was skeptical. I thought, "How is tapping on my face going to stop this inferno inside me?" But I was so miserable, so terrified, I figured, what did I have to lose? A few minutes of looking silly? Small price to pay if it offered even a flicker of relief.

The first few times, I felt nothing. Or maybe I felt more agitated, because I was so focused on how it wasn't working. But I kept at it. Because when you're in that place, you cling to anything that might help. I started with simple statements, like "Even though I'm terrified right now, I deeply and completely accept myself." Or, "Even though my body feels like it's vibrating apart, I choose to feel safe."

And slowly, subtly, something shifted. It wasn't a sudden, miraculous cure. It wasn't like the kundalini stopped. But the panic around it lessened. The sheer terror started to dim, just a little. The hyper-vigilance would sometimes soften. It gave me a moment, a tiny space, to breathe. It was like a dimmer switch for the panic button in my brain. Know what I mean? It allowed me to create a little bit of distance between myself and the overwhelming sensations.

It wasn't a silver bullet for the kundalini itself, but it was a lifeboat for the emotional tsunami that comes with it. And believe me, that emotional tsunami is often what pushes you to the brink. Managing the fear, the anxiety, the despair - that's where you get your footing back. That's where you find the strength to keep going. The Mindfulness and Acceptance Workbook for Anxiety (paid link) also has some good tools for this, even if it's not specifically about kundalini. The principles of acceptance and working with difficult sensations are incredibly valuable.

The Nuance: Tapping Won't "Fix" Kundalini, But It Helps You Live With It

Here's the crucial distinction: tapping isn't going to stop your kundalini awakening or crisis. It's not going to turn off the energy. Kundalini is a deep, powerful process of transformation, and once it's started, it's pretty much on its own schedule. Books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) can give you a better understanding of the process itself.

What tapping *can* do, and what it did for me, is help you manage the intense, often debilitating symptoms that come with it. It helps you regulate your nervous system. It helps you process the trauma and fear that get unearthed during an awakening. Because let's be real, a kundalini crisis often brings up every single unresolved trauma, every hidden fear, every shadow aspect you've ever had. It's a brutal clearing out process.

So, when you're feeling overwhelmed by a particular sensation - maybe intense heat in your spine, or a feeling of something trying to escape your head, or uncontrollable shaking - you can tap on the fear, the anxiety, the discomfort associated with that sensation. You don't try to tap away the kundalini itself. You tap on your *reaction* to it. Does that land?

Practical Tapping for Kundalini Crisis

So, how do you actually do it? You don't need to be an expert. Just follow these basic steps:

  1. Identify the Problem: What's the most pressing issue right now? Is it the overwhelming fear? The physical discomfort? The feeling of losing control? Be specific. "I'm terrified I'm going insane." "My body feels like it's burning." "I can't stop shaking."
  2. Rate the Intensity: On a scale of 0 to 10, how intense is that feeling right now? 10 being the absolute worst.
  3. The Setup Phrase: This is where you acknowledge the problem but also state a self-acceptance phrase. You say it three times while tapping on the "karate chop" point (the fleshy part on the outside of your hand, below your pinky finger). Examples:
    • "Even though I feel this intense fear that I'm going crazy, I deeply and completely accept myself."
    • "Even though my body is vibrating so strongly I can't stand it, I deeply and completely accept myself."
    • "Even though I'm so angry this is happening to me, I deeply and completely accept myself."
  4. The Tapping Sequence: Now, you tap through the sequence of points, repeating a reminder phrase for each point. The reminder phrase is usually just a shortened version of your problem.
    • Eyebrow: "This intense fear."
    • Side of Eye: "I'm going crazy."
    • Under Eye: "This terror."
    • Under Nose: "Can't stop it."
    • Chin: "This overwhelming feeling."
    • Collarbone: "This intense fear."
    • Under Arm: "All this panic."
    • Top of Head: "I'm so scared."
  5. Repeat and Re-evaluate: Go through the sequence a few times. Then, take a deep breath and re-evaluate your intensity. Has it gone down? Even if it's just from a 10 to an 8, that's progress. You keep tapping until the intensity reduces significantly, or you feel a shift.

You can also tap on positive affirmations once the intensity of the negative emotion has come down. For instance, "I choose to feel safe," or "I am capable of handling this." But honestly, when you're in crisis, focusing on the negative and acknowledging it is often more effective first. You can't bypass the pain; you have to go through it, and tapping can be a tool to help you move through it with less resistance.

The Caveats and Warnings

Okay, so I've told you how it can help. But I'm not going to pretend it's a magic wand. Here are some things you need to be aware of:

  • It's a Tool, Not a Cure: Tapping is a self-regulation tool. It helps manage symptoms. It doesn't stop the kundalini process itself. That process is going to run its course. Your job is to learn to live with it, integrate it, and minimize the suffering along the way.
  • It Can Bring Things Up: Sometimes, when you start tapping on intense emotions, more intense emotions can surface. That's normal. It means you're actually getting to the root of things. If it feels too much, stop, take a break, or try tapping on "Even though this is too much, I choose to be safe."
  • Get Support: Tapping can be done alone, but it's not a substitute for professional help. If you're going through a kundalini crisis, you absolutely need a support system. That could be a therapist who understands spiritual emergency, a spiritual teacher, or a trusted friend who can hold space for you. Don't try to tough this out completely alone. I did too much of that, and it made everything harder.
  • Listen to Your Body: If something feels wrong or too intense while tapping, stop. Your body is your guide. You're already dealing with a lot of intense sensations; you don't need to push yourself further.

So yeah, when you're sitting there in the dark, feeling like your world is crumbling, and you're wondering if this weird tapping thing can help - my honest answer is yes, it can. It won't make the kundalini disappear, but it can be a steady hand in the storm. It can help you find moments of calm, moments where the terror isn't quite so deafening. And in a kundalini crisis, those moments are gold. They're what allow you to breathe, to regroup, to find your center, and to remember that you're still in there somewhere, even amidst the chaos.

You're not broken, even if it feels like it. You're going through something incredibly intense, something that most people won't ever understand. But you're not alone in it. Keep looking for tools, keep looking for support, and keep tapping. You'll get through this. I promise.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
